title: Sampling methods
description: Practice identifying which sampling method was used in statistical studies, and why it might make sense to use one sampling method over another.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each student at a school has a student identification number. Counselors have a computer generate $50$ random identification numbers, and the students associated with those numbers are asked to take a survey.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
No groups were used, so this is *not* **stratified** or **cluster** sampling.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Every group of $50$ students in the entire school had the same chance to be selected.

The answer:

This is a simple random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A teacher puts students' names in a hat and chooses without looking to get a sample of $3$ students.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
No groups were used, so this is *not* **stratified** or **cluster** sampling.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Every group of $3$ students in the class had the same chance to be selected.

The answer:

This is a simple random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each law firm in one state registers its phone number with the state court system. An employee of the state court system uses a computer to select $500$ random registered phone numbers, and the law firms associated with those numbers will be selected for an audit.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
No groups were used, so this is *not* **stratified** or **cluster** sampling.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Every group of $500$ phone numbers in the populations had the same chance to be selected.

The answer:

This is a simple random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A manager associated each employee's name with a number on one ball in a container, then drew balls without looking to select a sample of $5$ employees.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
No groups were used, so this is *not* **stratified** or **cluster** sampling.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Every group of $5$ employees in the population had the same chance to be selected.

The answer:

This is a simple random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A quality control worker at a factory selects the first $10$ items she sees as her sample for the day.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The employee is choosing a sample that is readily available but not necessarily random.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a convenience sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A researcher polls people as they walk by on the street.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The researcher is choosing a sample that is readily available but not necessarily random.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a convenience sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A politician asks his neighbors their opinions about a controversial issue.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The politician is choosing a sample that is readily available but not necessarily random.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a convenience sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A professor asks the first $5$ students who arrive to class to participate in a research study about young adult sleep patterns.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The professor is choosing a sample that is readily available but not necessarily random.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a convenience sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant leaves comment cards on all of its tables to learn about customer satisfaction. Each comment card is a brief survey that customers can fill out if they choose to.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The restaurant asks all customers to respond, but the customers themselves choose whether or not to be in the sample.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a voluntary response sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A TV show host asks his viewers to visit his website and respond to an online poll.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The host asks all viewers to respond, but the viewers themselves choose whether or not to be in the sample.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a voluntary response sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A store prints a request on each receipt asking customers to fill out a satisfaction survey online if they are willing.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The store asks all customers to respond, but the customers themselves choose whether or not to be in the sample.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a voluntary response sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A support hotline asks callers to stay on the line after they have completed their call to respond to a short survey.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The hotline asks all callers to respond, but the callers themselves choose whether or not to be in the sample.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The answer:

None of the above (this is a voluntary response sample)

assessment_items.question_content:
A school chooses $3$ randomly selected athletes from each of its sports teams to participate in a survey about athletics at the school.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of athletes had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The athletes were divided into groups (teams) and *some* athletes were selected from *each* group.

The answer:

This is a stratified random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student council surveys $100$ students by taking random samples of $25$ freshmen, $25$ sophomores, $25$ juniors, and $25$ seniors.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of $100$ students had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The students were divided into groups (freshmen, sophomores, juniors, seniors) and *some* students were selected from *each* group.

The answer:

This is a stratified random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large company surveys $100$ employees by taking random samples of $10$ managers and $90$ non-managerial employees.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of $100$ employees had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The employees were divided into groups (managers and non-managerial employees) and *some* employees were selected from *each* group.

The answer:

This is a stratified random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A truck manufacturer selects $3$ trucks at random from each of $6$ models for safety testing.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of $18$ trucks had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The trucks were divided into groups (models) and *some* trucks were selected from *each* group.

The answer:

This is a stratified random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A principal orders t-shirts and wants to check some of them to make sure they were printed properly. She randomly selects $2$ of the $10$ boxes of shirts and checks every shirt in those $2$ boxes.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of shirts had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The shirts were divided into groups (boxes) and the principal sampled *every* shirt from *some* of the groups.

The answer:

This is a cluster random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
An airline company wants to survey its customers one day, so they randomly select $5$ flights that day and survey every passenger on those flights.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of passengers had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The passengers were divided into groups (flights) and the company sampled *every* passenger from *some* of the groups.

The answer:

This is a cluster random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A hospital wants to survey religious participants in their city about what they seek from a hospital chaplain, so they randomly select $5$ religious meetings in the city and survey every participant in those meetings.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of religious participants had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The religious participants were divided into groups (religious meetings) and the hospital sampled *every* participant from *some* of the groups.

The answer:

This is a cluster random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Inspectors for a hospital chain with multiple locations randomly select some of their locations for a cleanliness check of their operating rooms. The inspectors check every operating room in the hospitals that were chosen.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of operating rooms had the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The operating rooms were divided into groups (hospital locations) and inspectors sampled *every* operating room from *some* of the groups.

The answer:

This is a cluster random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Security workers at an airport randomly choose one of the first $50$ people to pass through a checkpoint for extra security screening. After that person, they choose every $50^\text{th}$ person who passes through for extra screening as well. 

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of people has the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 2]]
The people enter the checkpoint in some order. The security workers randomly select one of the first $k$ people, and then they choose every $k^\text{th}$ person thereafter.

The answer:

This is a systematic random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A large bakery mass produces cakes on an assembly line. Each shift, a quality control expert randomly selects one of the first ten finished cakes, and every tenth cake thereafter. Employees weigh those cakes and give the cakes a detailed visual check.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of cakes has the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The cakes go through the assembly line in some order. The employees randomly select one of the first $k$ cakes, and then they choose every $k^\text{th}$ cake thereafter.

The answer:

This is a systematic random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A manager at a pizza shop wants to start visually checking finished pizzas, but she doesn't have time to check every pizza. She decides to check a random pizza in the first $20$ made each day, and then check every $20^\text{th}$ pizza thereafter.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of pizzas has the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The pizzas get made in some order. The manager randomly selects one of the first $k$ pizzas, and then she chooses every $k^\text{th}$ pizza thereafter.

The answer:

This is a systematic random sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Employees at a website want to select a sample of their users to ask for a donation. They randomly select one of the first $25$ users each day and show them a message asking for a donation. They also show the message to every $25^\text{th}$ user from that point on.

**What type of sample is this?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Not every subset of users has the same chance of being selected, so this is *not* a **simple random sample**.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The users visit the website in some order. The website randomly selects one of the first $k$ users, and then they choose every $k^\text{th}$ user thereafter.

The answer:

This is a systematic random sample.
